1.synthesis: Daniel 7 opens the second half of the book—the transition from court tales (chs. 1–6) to apocalyptic visions (chs. 7–12)—with the most comprehensive symbolic vision of world history in the Hebrew Bible. In the first year of Belshazzar (ca. 553 BCE), Daniel sees four great beasts emerge from a sea churned by the four winds of heaven. The sea (yamma rabba) is simultaneously the Mediterranean and the primordial chaos-ocean of ANE cosmology: the beasts rise from the realm of disorder. The four beasts represent four successive world empires. The first—a lion with eagle’s wings that are plucked and the creature made to stand like a man, given a human heart—is Babylon: the winged lion (lamassu) was ubiquitous in Babylonian palace art, and the humanization of the beast may allude to Nebuchadnezzar’s animal transformation and restoration in ch. 4. The second—a bear raised on one side with three ribs in its mouth—is Medo-Persia, the lopsided elevation reflecting Persian dominance over Media. The third—a leopard with four wings and four heads—is Greece: the leopard’s speed captures Alexander’s historically unprecedented campaign pace (334–323 BCE), and the four heads represent the Diadochi kingdoms after Alexander’s death. The fourth beast—deliberately left without animal analogy (different from all the others), with iron teeth and bronze claws, devouring and crushing everything—is Rome in the dominant interpretation. From this beast’s ten horns emerges a small horn (qeren zeira) that uproots three and speaks great words, pointing to Antiochus IV Epiphanes in the near-term reading and to an eschatological adversary in the far horizon of the vision.
1.historical_context: The four-empire scheme had ancient precedent in Greek and Persian literature (Hesiod’s ages of gold, silver, bronze, and iron; the Persian tradition of four world kingdoms), but Daniel gives it redemptive-historical content: these are not cycles but a sequence building toward divine intervention. The vision is given in the first year of Belshazzar (ca. 553 BCE), approximately two years before the handwriting on the wall (ch. 5). The beast imagery draws on Babylonian and Ugaritic mythological traditions: the chaos-sea, the primordial monsters (Tiamat in the Babylonian creation myth; the sea-serpent Yam and the seven-headed Lotan/Leviathan in Ugaritic texts). Daniel recasts these as the theological grammar for human empire: what the nations call greatness, heaven sees as chaos-creatures. The Aramaic language of the vision (chs. 2:4b–7:28 are in Aramaic) places the empires-of-the-world section in the diplomatic-commercial language of the ancient Near East, while the Hebrew resumes with Daniel’s personal visions in chs. 8–12.
1.literary_structure: The four-beast sequence follows a chiastic escalation: the first three beasts are described briefly (vv. 4–6) and the fourth at length (v. 7), with the little horn requiring a second inquiry (vv. 19–26). The structure mirrors ch. 2’s four-metal statue: both use a quadripartite scheme, both show the fourth as most destructive, and both end with divine kingdom replacing human ones. Ch. 7 is ch. 2’s complement—the human perspective of ch. 2 (the statue you see from below) against the divine perspective of ch. 7 (the beasts you see from heaven). The movement from the description of the beasts (vv. 1–8) to the heavenly court scene (vv. 9–14) is a spatial shift from earth-level chaos to heaven-level sovereignty.
1.theological_application: The vision establishes a fundamental theological claim: what human societies experience as rising empires, heaven perceives as chaos-beasts. The greatness and terror of Babylon, Persia, Greece, and Rome are real—but their ultimate character is bestial rather than divine. This is not cynicism about human civilization but a theological correction of every empire’s self-divinizing narrative. Every empire presents itself as bringing order; the vision shows that from the divine vantage point, empires are themselves creatures of chaos. The implication for communities under empire is both sobering (do not trust empires) and comforting (the chaos-beasts are numbered and will be judged): resist the temptation to attribute ultimate significance to any political arrangement.
1.christology: The four-beast vision is the necessary context for the Son of Man vision that follows (Daniel 7:13–14): the four chaos-beasts set up the contrast with the one who comes like a son of man—a human figure, not a beast, receiving a kingdom that contrasts with everything the beasts represent. Jesus’ characteristic self-designation as “Son of Man” throughout the Gospels draws directly on this text, identifying himself as the figure who receives the eternal kingdom from the Ancient of Days. The beast imagery recurs in Revelation 13, where the sea-beast combines features of all four Daniel beasts—showing that the New Testament reads Daniel’s beasts as patterns fulfilled in the Roman empire and ultimately in the eschatological adversary whom Christ defeats.

9.synthesis: The throne scene of vv. 9–14 is the theological center not only of ch. 7 but of the entire second half of Daniel, and arguably one of the most important visionary texts in the Hebrew Bible. The Ancient of Days (ʿattiq yomaya, “advanced in days”) is seated on a throne of blazing fire with wheels of fire, clothed in white and with hair white as wool, a river of fire (nahar di-nur) flowing from before him, thousands upon thousands attending him. The heavenly court convenes, books are opened, and the little horn is slain. Then Daniel sees: “with the clouds of heaven there came one like a son of man (kebar enash)”—a human figure, in contrast to the four bestial empires—who approaches the Ancient of Days and is given dominion (shaltan), glory (yeqar), and a kingdom (malkhevah) that all peoples, nations, and languages serve. This dominion is eternal and indestructible. The contrast is absolute: the beasts rose from the sea by their own chaotic force and were stripped of dominion; the Son of Man comes on clouds and receives dominion as a gift from the throne. The vision answers the book’s deepest question—who ultimately rules?—with a scene of judicial transfer of cosmic authority from the Ancient of Days to the Son of Man.
9.historical_context: The title ʿattiq yomaya (Ancient of Days) is unique to Daniel in the OT and probably unique in ANE literature. The imagery—white garments, white hair, fiery throne with wheels—is drawn from and amplifies the throne-chariot vision of Ezekiel 1, where YHWH’s merkavah has wheels of fire and the likeness of a man above the throne. The heavenly court setting, with attendants and open books, reflects Second Temple Jewish development of the divine council tradition (cf. Psalm 82:1; Job 1:6). The river of fire connects to Deuteronomy 4:24 (YHWH as consuming fire) and anticipates the “lake of fire” of Revelation 20:10. The phrase kebar enash (“one like a son of man” / “one like a human being”) presents an interpretive crux: whether this figure is a corporate symbol (the faithful Israel / the saints), an individual Messiah, an angelic being (possibly Michael), or some combination that includes all three has been debated since antiquity. The NT’s christological use (Matthew 26:64) is the most influential application.
9.literary_structure: The throne scene (vv. 9–12) is followed by the Son of Man vision (vv. 13–14), creating a diptych: first the Judge, then the Recipient of judgment’s fruit. The judgment section (vv. 9–12) disposes of the little horn (v. 11) while preserving the first three beasts in a diminished condition (v. 12)—a historically perceptive detail: the empires of Babylon, Persia, and Greece were superseded but continued in attenuated forms. The Son of Man scene (vv. 13–14) is the climax toward which the entire chapter builds; its chiastic structure mirrors the beast-description: four beasts descended from chaos ↔ one Son of Man ascends to the throne. The cloud-riding imagery places the Son of Man in the position typically occupied by YHWH in OT theophanies, where YHWH rides the clouds as his chariot (Psalm 68:4; Isaiah 19:1).
9.theological_application: The throne scene answers the pastoral crisis of exile: if the chaos-beasts rule, is YHWH on his throne? The vision’s answer is direct—the Ancient of Days has been seated (ramiyu korsavan, thrones were set in place) throughout; the divine court has always been in session. The books of judgment have been open all along; the little horn’s boasting is heard by the Judge and is the measure of its own condemnation. Communities under persecution or imperial oppression need this vision: the injustice witnessed on earth is simultaneously being adjudicated in the heavenly court. The transfer of the kingdom to the Son of Man grounds the community’s hope not in political reversal (though that will come) but in the eternal verdict already being executed in heaven.
9.christology: The Son of Man vision is the single most important OT text for understanding Jesus’ self-understanding. He applies Daniel 7:13 to himself at his trial before the Sanhedrin (Matthew 26:64: “you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power, and coming on the clouds of heaven”), provoking the charge of blasphemy—because the Sanhedrin understood that claiming to be the kebar enash was claiming to receive divine authority. The universal dominion given to the Son of Man (“all peoples, nations, and languages shall serve him”) is the content of the Great Commission (Matthew 28:18: “all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me”). The eternal, indestructible kingdom of the Son of Man is the kingdom of God that Jesus announces as both present and coming.

15.synthesis: Daniel’s inner response to the throne-vision and the beast-vision is profound distress: “my spirit was troubled within me (etkarev ruchi ana Daniel begov nidbeka)”—the Aramaic nidneh (sheath or body) suggests the spirit disturbed within its bodily housing. He approaches one of those standing by the throne and requests the yatsivata—reliable interpretation, certain meaning. The interpretation given in vv. 17–18 is deliberately summary: the four great beasts are four kings who shall arise from the earth, but the saints of the Most High (qadishei ʿIllaya) shall receive the kingdom and possess it forever, forever and ever. This three-verse interpretation does not explain the individual beasts or the little horn—that detail comes in the second inquiry of vv. 19–27. The first interpretation focuses entirely on the structural point: kings from earth, but the kingdom to the saints from heaven. The contrast between from the earth and receive from the Most High is the interpretive key. The saints do not conquer the kingdom through political or military action; they receive it as a gift from the Ancient of Days, just as the Son of Man received it.
15.historical_context: The identity of qadishei ʿIllaya (saints of the Most High) has been debated since antiquity. Three main interpretations: (1) angelic beings (the language of “Most High” is associated with angels in Qumran texts; Daniel 4:17 speaks of “watchers” as holy ones); (2) faithful Israel (the community that maintained covenant faithfulness through persecution); (3) a corporate body that includes both the angelic realm and faithful Israel in solidarity. The parallelism with the Son of Man receiving the kingdom in vv. 13–14 suggests that the saints receive what the Son of Man receives, and that the Son of Man’s reception is the basis for theirs—a pattern the NT will exploit in its ecclesiology (the church as the body of the Son of Man who receives the kingdom). The Aramaic phrase alam v’almin (“forever and ever”, literally “age and ages”) first appears here and becomes a liturgical formula in early Jewish and Christian worship.
15.literary_structure: The four-verse unit (vv. 15–18) functions as a hermeneutical hinge: it provides the minimum structural interpretation before Daniel’s second, more detailed inquiry in v. 19. The structure is request (vv. 15–16) and minimal answer (vv. 17–18). The economy of the answer is notable: the interpreter does not rehearse the beast imagery but leaps directly to the outcome, because the outcome (saints receive the kingdom forever) is the point to be fixed in the reader’s memory before the troubling details of persecution are elaborated. This order—outcome first, then the difficult path to the outcome—is a rhetorical strategy of pastoral comfort: know where this is going before learning what will happen on the way.
15.theological_application: The summary interpretation models a principle of apocalyptic reading: the details of historical tribulation are meant to be understood within the framework of the outcome, not the outcome within the framework of the tribulation. The church has often reversed this, reading prophetic texts primarily for their chronological detail and losing the forest for the trees. The vision’s first interpretation insists: whatever the kings and beasts do (the details), the saints receive the kingdom (the frame). This outcome-first reading is pastorally essential for communities under persecution: the end of the story is already declared; what remains is to live faithfully through the middle chapters.
15.christology: The saints receiving the kingdom “forever, forever and ever” is the communal dimension of what the Son of Man receives individually in vv. 13–14. Paul’s theology of co-inheritance develops this: those who are “in Christ” share in his inheritance (Romans 8:17: “heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ”). The saints of the Most High who receive the kingdom are those who are joined to the Son of Man who received it first. The alam v’almin (forever and ever) that describes the saints’ possession of the kingdom becomes the doxological refrain of Revelation 5:13 and 22:5: the redeemed reign with the Lamb for ever and ever.

19.pericope_label: The Meaning of the Fourth Beast
19.synthesis: Daniel’s second inquiry focuses specifically on the fourth beast and the little horn, which pressed on him beyond what the summary interpretation resolved. The elaboration reveals: the little horn made war with the saints (kazba im-qadishein) and prevailed against them, until the Ancient of Days came and judgment was given for the saints and the saints possessed the kingdom. The sequence is precise: persecution → divine arrival → judgment for saints → kingdom possession. The little horn will speak against the Most High (millaya tzad ʿIllaya yimaleil), wear out the saints (yibla qadishei ʿIllaya), and attempt to change times and law (yisbar lehashna zimnin v’dat)—a totalizing program of theological subversion—and the saints will be given into his hand for a time, times, and half a time (ʿad ʿiddan v’iddanin u’felaq ʿiddan). Then the heavenly court removes his dominion and gives the kingdom under all heaven to the people of the saints of the Most High: his dominion is everlasting and all rulers will serve and obey him. Daniel’s thoughts greatly trouble him and his color changes. The vision is psychosomatically costly: what is revealed about the path to the kingdom is not comfortable, even when the outcome is secure.
19.historical_context: The “change of times and law” (v. 25) finds its historical expression in Antiochus IV Epiphanes’ decrees of 168–167 BCE: abolition of the Sabbath, prohibition of circumcision, and compulsory observance of Greek religious festivals (1 Maccabees 1:41–64). The “time, times, and half a time” (3½ times) corresponds to the period of the Antiochene persecution (167–164 BCE, approximately 3½ years), connecting to the 1,260 days of Revelation 12:6 and the 42 months of Revelation 13:5. The “wearing out” (yibla) of the saints is an unusual verb meaning to exhaust by attrition—not outright slaughter but the grinding down of endurance through prolonged pressure. The heavenly court’s session (vdin yitiv, judgment shall sit) in v. 26 reverses the little horn’s dominion through the same legal process that adjudicated the beasts: the court that condemned the beasts now strips the horn.
19.literary_structure: The second inquiry and response (vv. 19–27) elaborates the first (vv. 15–18) with the focus on the fourth beast and the little horn. The structure moves from Daniel’s inquiry (vv. 19–22) through the angelic response (vv. 23–27) to Daniel’s response (v. 28). The angelic response in vv. 23–27 mirrors the vision sequence but reframes it in interpretive rather than visionary language. The climactic v. 27 inverts the little horn’s power-catalog: where the horn spoke great words and prevailed, the saints receive greatness; where the horn wore out the saints, rulers serve the saints. Daniel’s final alarm and changed color (v. 28) marks the vision’s experiential cost: the weight of revelation is proportional to the weight of what is revealed.
19.theological_application: The sequence “little horn prevails against saints—until the Ancient of Days came” (vv. 21–22) is the most honest statement in the visionary literature about the saints’ vulnerability. The oracle does not promise invincibility; it promises vindication. The saints are genuinely worn out, genuinely overcome for a season. The “time, times, and half a time” is a bounded period—a deliberate 3½, the midpoint of 7 (the complete number), which communicates that the tribulation is limited without specifying its exact length. Communities under persecution need both the honesty (the little horn will prevail for a season) and the limit (only a season, bounded by divine decree). The wearing-out is real; the court is already in session.
19.christology: The “wearing out of the saints” by the little horn finds its christological center in the Passion: Jesus himself was worn out, given into the hands of the adversary, prevailed against in the short term. The 3½ days between crucifixion and resurrection is the personal enactment of the “time, times, and half a time”—the bounded period during which the adversary appears to prevail. The Ancient of Days’ coming to vindicate the saints is anticipated by the resurrection, which is YHWH’s arrival in judgment for the Son of Man and, through him, for all the saints. The kingdom under all heaven given to “the people of the saints of the Most High” (v. 27) is the new creation in which the risen Christ reigns and his people reign with him (2 Timothy 2:12: “if we endure, we will also reign with him”).
